content.full_text:
RAVI MERTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
March 5, 1997
Robert P. Marshall, Pharm. D.
California Pharmacists Association
1112 I Street, Suite 300
Sacramento, California 95814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-048
Dear Mr. Marshall:
This letter is a response to your request on behalf ofthe California Pharmacists
Association for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Please
bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which
may afready have taken place. Further, this letter is based on the facts as they have been
presented to us. The Commission does not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because you seek guidance on the conduct of a series of
upcoming events and cannot provide us with a full and detailed account of all the facts and
circumstances that may be pertinent to any particular occasion, we are treating this inquiry as a
request for informal assistance, as required under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed).
QUESTION
Does the recent enactment of Proposition 208 limit expenditures by a political action
conmiittee in support of grassroots educational events designed to bring members of a
professional association together with state and local elected officials, to discuss matters of
professional concern to association members?
CONCLUSION
Proposition 208 may affect the Association’s activities in two areas. First, any valuable
goods or services provided to public officials in connection with their appearance at these events
may be characterized as contributions subject to the timing, source and amount limitations of
Proposition 208. In addition, it is possible that persons devoting substantial time and effort to
1 Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-048
Page 2
Proposition 208. In addition, it is possible that persons devoting substantial time and effort to
these events might fall within the new defmition of “lobbyist” introduced by Proposition 208.
FACTS
The California Pharmacists Association (the “Association”) is an organization whose
membership includes pharmacists practicing in California. In 1996 the Association began to
conduct gatherings which you describe as grassroots educational events. The Association
expects to continue and to expand these meetings in 1997. Event funding from the Association’s
political action committee will be used to organize and finance these occasions, which will be
attended by local pharmacists on the one hand, and by state and local public officials on the
other. The purpose of these events will be to afford an opportunity to pharmacists to interact with
elected officials from their cities, counties, school boards and state legislative districts, to educate
them about issues of concern to pharmacists, to discuss legislative measures, and more generally
to involve pharmacists in the political process. Although the Association retains a contract
lobbying firm, no representative from that firm will be present at the events in question.
ANALYSIS
The Political Reform Act was adopted by California voters through the initiative process
in 1974, and in 1996 was substantially amended by another initiative measure, Proposition 208.
Proposition 208 introduces changes to the Act that have the potential to impact the Association’s
plans for grassroots educational meetings in two areas. Officials attending these events may, in
so doing, become recipients of contributions from the Association’s political action committee
subject to new limitations imposed by Proposition 208. Secondly, changes in the definition of
“lobbyist” may result in registration requirements and ancillary prohibitions applicable to persons
whose activities would not necessarily have made them “lobbyists” under the prior Act. Because
your question is limited to the effects of Proposition 208, we do not discuss questions arising
solely under the law as it existed prior to enactment of Proposition 208.
If the Association’s political action committee funds travel arrangements or
accommodations, meals, refreshments, entertainment or other valuable benefits, its expenditures
may be characterized as contributions to the public officials who attend these gatherings. Since
Proposition 208 introduces numerous limitations on the permissible amounts, timing, and
sources of contributions, it is now particularly important to characterize accurately any benefits
ancillary to attendance at these events.
Proposition 208 provides at Section 85202 that existing definitions and provisions ofthe
Act continue in effect unless specifically superseded by the terms of Proposition 208. As a
result, Section 82015, which provides the definition of “contribution,” governs the question here,


File No. A-97-048
Page 3
along with companion regulations and advice letters issued under prior law.
2 Under Section
82015, “contribution” includes any payment, forgiveness of a loan, payment of a loan by a third
party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made
for political purposes. A payment is made for political purposes if it is: (1) for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or
election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure; x (2) is
received by or made at the behest of a candidate.
Regulation 18215 (copy enclosed) states with particularity the kinds of payments that
will, or will not, be considered contributions. Subsection (b)(3) includes within the term: “[amy
goods or services received by or behested by a candidate or committee at no charge or at a
discount from the fair market value, unless the discount is given in the regular course ofbusiness
to members of the public.” Elected officials are considered “candidates” under the Act by virtue
of Sections 82007 and 84214, which provide that a candidate for elected office remains a
“candidate” after assuming office, for however long he or she has filing obligations under
Chapter 4 of the Act. (Schmidt Advice Letter, No. A-96-072.) Such filing obligations include
campaign statements required under Sections 84200 and 84215.
As a “candidate,” any payment to an elected official is presumed to be for political
purposes unless a specific exception applies. (Danner Advice Letter, No. A-96-039.) Further,
Regulation 18225.7(a) (copy inclosed) provides that a contribution is made “at the behest of’ a
candidate or official when the contribution is made “under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or in concert with...” that candidate or official. Officials
who accept the Association’s invitations to attend its educational events plainly act in
cooperation and in concert with the Association.
It should by now be apparent that, unless a specific exclusion applies, anything of value
given to a public official at the Association’s educational events may be characterized as a
“contribution,” subject to the limitations of amount, time, and source imposed by Proposition
208. Specific exceptions to the definition of “contribution” are given within Regulation 18215,
and we urge you to contact us if you are in doubt as to the appropriate characterization of any
item or service of value that you may furnish to officials attending your gatherings.
2 Also, please note that any particular expenditure of the PAC must be permissible under the “personal
use” laws. (Sections 89510-89518.)
Alternatively, goods or services ofvalue may be characterized as “gif” to the official. Proposition 208
has not changed the law on gifts to public officials, but the distinction between gift and contribution has assumed a
new significance with the introduction of comprehensive limitations on contributions.


File No. A-97-048
Page 4
The second critical point of contact between Proposition 208 and the Association’s
planned gatherings is the new definition of “lobbyist” at Section 82039, which was substantially
rewritten by Proposition 208. The Commission is presently considering regulations to implement
and clarify this new definition, and it is not yet possible to furnish you with detailed guidance.
You should be especially aware that a lobbyist may now be defmed as anyone whose “principal
duties” as an employee are to communicate directly or through agents with public officials for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action. Regulations implementing the older
definition, and advice letters treating these matters under former law, may no longer be reliable
guides.
The Association plainly contemplates that its member pharmacists wifi communicate with
public officials attending these events, for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative
action. It is possible that employees ofthe Association or its political action committee might fit
within the new definition of “lobbyist” ifthey devote substantial amounts oftime to organizing
the events, contacting public officials to arrange for their appearances, andlor preparing the
pharmacists who will ultimately communicate on matters Of interest to the Association. Until the
appropriate application of new Section 82039 can be decided, and companion regulations
adopted, the Association and its political action committee should pay close attention to work
assignments relating to these educational events.
If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
•
•
By:
Lawrence T. Woodlock
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LTW:ak
